source.title: Temporal Variation in Danger Drives Antipredator Behavior: The Predation Risk Allocation Hypothesis.

confidence_rationale: Canonical predation-risk-allocation hypothesis paper with thousands of citations and decades of empirical testing. Direct theoretical foundation for the R4 calibration question. Confidence high because the paper is foundational; reduced from highest only because it is theory not direct empirical calibration data.
failure_signatures: If ARC-062 weak reading on SD-054 produces antipredator allocation that is INVARIANT across hazard density (i.e., reef_visit_fraction the same regardless of how many hazards are active), the risk-allocation hypothesis predicts this is a failure of context-discrimination — the agent is treating all hazard regimes as equivalent and not gating policy on the relative-risk variable. Diagnostic: vary SD-054 hazard density across arms and measure whether reef_visit_fraction tracks the variation.

Under chronic high-risk regimes, Lima-Bednekoff predicts antipredator effort REDUCES because the animal must feed sometime — if ARC-062 maintains high refuge-use under chronic high-risk and starves the agent, the architectural commitment is suppressing the risk-allocation logic in a biologically inappropriate way.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Temporal variability in predation risk leaves animals with the problem of allocating feeding and antipredator efforts across different risk situations. Animals should exhibit greatest antipredator behaviour in high-risk situations that are brief and infrequent. Animals should allocate more antipredator effort to high-risk and more feeding to low-risk, with this difference increasing with the relative degree of risk. However, under chronic high-risk, animals must feed despite the risk — antipredator effort in low-risk situations may drop to low levels as the animal allocates as much feeding as possible to brief periods of low risk. A common experimental protocol of low-risk maintenance with brief high-risk pulse OVERESTIMATES antipredator intensity expected in field or chronic exposure.
mapping.ree_translation: Direct theoretical foundation for R4 (Phase 2 falsifier acceptance threshold calibration). The risk-allocation hypothesis predicts that an animal's refuge-vs-forage allocation is a FUNCTION of the temporal risk pattern, not a fixed ratio. Three load-bearing predictions for ARC-062 + SD-054 calibration: (1) Allocation should depend on hazard density and pattern (chronic vs pulsed). The arbitrary EXQ-522 hand-coded heuristic at reef_visit_fraction in [0.3, 0.7] is biologically defensible only if SD-054's hazard density matches the regime where the heuristic was tuned. The Phase 2 falsifier should test ARC-062's allocation across a hazard-density gradient, not at a single point. (2) Under chronic high-risk regimes, refuge-use REDUCES (not increases) because the agent has no choice but to feed — counterintuitive prediction that distinguishes architecturally honest gated-policy from a naive 'always-flee-when-hazard-present' policy. (3) Pulsed-risk paradigms overestimate antipredator behaviour. The SD-054 substrate uses persistent reef and persistent food-attracted hazards (not pulses), so the calibration target should be from chronic-risk empirical work, not pulsed-risk work.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Lima-Bednekoff 1999 is a theoretical paper, not direct quantitative calibration data. It predicts the SHAPE of the allocation function (how it should change with risk pattern) but does not provide a numerical 'X% refuge / Y% forage' ratio for any specific hazard regime. Direct calibration requires empirical companion entries (Sundell 2004, Balaban-Feld 2019, Eccard 2020 in this directory). The theory is also species-general; specific calibration for fish-in-coral-reef ecology may differ in the relevant constants from the rodent-and-vole literature where most direct empirical tests have been run.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical paper in *American Naturalist* deriving optimal antipredator allocation as a function of temporal risk pattern; species-general.

confidence_components.notes: Foundational theoretical paper, extensively cited and replicated. Mapping fidelity high: the theoretical structure transfers cleanly to ARC-062's gated-policy + risk-context discriminator architecture. Transfer risk low because the theory is species-general and the SD-054 substrate's reef-vs-forage partition is exactly the kind of allocation problem the hypothesis addresses.
